channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Talking (and Not Talking) Taiwan
item.description: A tale of two readouts.

item.title: Day 1 of Beijing Summit Produces No Big Wins for Trump
item.description: From the Iran war to trade, the U.S. president failed to secure major concessions from his counterpart.

item.title: Trump, Xi Hold High-Stakes Summit in Beijing
item.description: From the Iran war to trade, the U.S. president failed to secure major concessions from his counterpart.

item.title: Mexico’s daft plan to cut the school year for the World Cup
item.description: Does Claudia Sheinbaum really want to improve education?

item.title: Israel’s Lebanon Strategy Is Self-Defeating
item.description: A weakened state can’t disarm Hezbollah.

item.title: What Happened in India’s West Bengal?
item.description: The BJP decisively ousted longtime leaders in the state, but last-minute changes to the voter rolls have fueled allegations of foul play.

item.title: Transnational Repression of Hong Kongers in the UK Is Disturbingly Common
item.description: New survey data from Hong Kong Watch helps quantify the scale of the problem – including among Hong Kongers not engaged in public activism.

item.title: Trump-Xi Summit Leaves South Korea Navigating Familiar Pressures
item.description: For Seoul, what the leaders of the great superpowers left unsaid in Beijing may matter more than what they agreed on.

item.title: Tajikistan and China Sign Permanent Friendship Treaty
item.description: Rahmon's four-day state visit also produced more than $8 billion in projected investment deals and confirmed Beijing's displacement of Moscow as Dushanbe's principal economic patron.

item.title: Why India Is Growing Suspicious of Nepal’s New Government
item.description: India thought it had an agreement with the RSP leadership on its interests being accommodated.

item.title: Trump, Xi Seek ‘New Chapter in China-US Relations’
item.description: There was a lot of grand talk about stabilizing the relationship, and few deliverables.

item.title: A Defining Moment for Central Asia
item.description: Central Asia has long been dismissed as too risky, too isolated, and too politically unstable to attract international investors. But a landmark IPO by Uzbekistan’s national investment fund may finally change that, signaling that one of the world’s fastest-growing regions is eager and ready to attract global capital.

item.title: As Uzbekistan Prepares for the World Cup, What’s Next for Uzbek Football?
item.description: The current decade could become the first golden era of Uzbek football, exemplified by two youth championships and participation at the upcoming World Cup.

item.title: Indonesia’s president is jeopardising the economy and democracy
item.description: Prabowo Subianto is too spendthrift and too authoritarian

item.title: Not all Donald Trump’s peacemaking boasts are empty
item.description: But to end Congo’s terrible war, America must remain neutral

item.title: Mothers who cannot breastfeed have been given terrible advice
item.description: Medicine has neglected mammary glands for too long

item.title: Indonesia, the biggest Muslim-majority country, is on a risky path
item.description: Prabowo Subianto is eroding its finances—and its democracy

item.title: Meet Anno Takahiro, founder of Japan’s hottest political party
item.description: The software engineer and sci-fi writer wants to use AI to reshape politics

item.title: Can a Chinese EV-maker reinvent itself as a robot firm?
item.description: An interview with the boss of Xpeng

item.title: China’s tea brands want to conquer America, Starbucks-style
item.description: A major competition is brewing

item.title: What Donald Trump could learn from the UFC
item.description: Maybe the fighters he has invited to the White House will supply some moral uplift

item.title: America’s new counter-terrorism strategy is a partisan polemic
item.description: It does not seem designed to make the country safer

item.title: New York looks set to lower a big barrier to building
item.description: Sex and the environmental-review process

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Politics
Business
Peter Magyar takes office pledging to clean up Hungary’s mess
item.description: EU funds blocked because of corruption under Viktor Orban are his priority

item.title: Armenia‘s election will test its leader’s pivot to the West
item.description: Let down by Russia, Nikol Pashinyan wants to make up with Azerbaijan and join the EU

item.title: Socialism is being left behind in Europe
item.description: The workers’ parties aren’t working

item.title: To understand European voters’ anger, look at their rent bills
item.description: Rent-control policies are making Europe’s housing shortage worse

item.title: The strange Japanese companies minting money from AI
item.description: What the creator of MSG and the world’s biggest toilet-maker have in common

item.title: Introducing “Velocity pivot”
item.description: The corporate world’s Lorem ipsum

item.title: Samsung Electronics has staged a stunning comeback
item.description: But political trouble is brewing

item.title: Companies are making big bucks from immigration crackdowns
item.description: And startups are piling in with whizzy new technologies

item.title: Macron turns to English-speaking Africa
item.description: Why France is betting on business to shake off its colonial baggage

item.title: A bombshell leak threatens Flávio Bolsonaro’s election bid
item.description: Text and voice messages show a close connection between Flávio and Daniel Vorcaro, a disgraced banker

item.title: Why measles is returning to the Americas
item.description: Falling vaccination coverage is letting a deadly virus back in

item.title: Samsung has staged a stunning comeback
item.description: But political trouble is brewing

item.title: Forced Labor Remains Central to Turkmenistan’s Cotton Harvest
item.description: Despite small steps in 2023 and 2024, the 2025 harvest reportedly saw a complete return to the mobilization of state employees into the fields.

item.title: Why Keir Starmer Will Fall
item.description: The British prime minister’s timid reign will serve as a how-not-to guide in the exercise of power.

item.title: The World Must Not Turn Its Back on Vaccination
item.description: Childhood immunization is the world’s most cost-effective public-health intervention, with a single vaccine dose costing less than $2 to administer—even in active conflict zones. But local delivery in the hardest-to-reach places, where zero-dose children are concentrated, requires investment in models that depend on local partners.

item.title: The World After the Iran War
item.description: Not all wars lead to better international orders. The Iran war launched by the United States and Israel is likely to prove particularly damaging in this respect, which will become all too obvious at this week’s US-China summit in Beijing, where Chinese President Xi Jinping has been given the upper hand.

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Why America still argues about 1965
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, on the unfinished battles of the civil-rights era

item.title: Sir Keir Starmer has failed abjectly. He should go
item.description: Britain is not ungovernable—it just needs better governance

item.title: India’s legendary hill towns are sinking
item.description: Overdevelopment threatens many Himalayan states

item.title: Oil markets have won a surprise reprieve
item.description: It is unlikely to last

item.title: Oil prices could soon rise convulsively
item.description: The present tranquility will not last

item.title: The Gulf war will change Asia for good
item.description: Energy-shocked countries are determined never to be hostage again

item.title: Prepare for an AI jobs apocalypse
item.description: It is not here yet. But governments should lay a safety-net

item.title: How to share the AI windfall
item.description: Are taxes enough?

item.title: The jobs apocalypse: a (very) short history
item.description: Mass unemployment induced by AI would be unprecedented

item.title: America’s Superpower Suicide
item.description: Donald Trump’s disastrous war in Iran underscores that the guiding principle of his foreign policy is superpower suicide. But this is a symptom of a deeper condition: the democratic distortions and drastic inequalities that have enabled world-historic levels of strategic buffoonery.

item.title: Trade or Taiwan? Trump and Xi struggle to set the terms
item.description: At a summit in Beijing, business deals may mask divisions on Iran, Ukraine and AI

item.title: Looking For Oil in All the Wrong Places
item.description: The Australian government has gone looking for oil in East and Southeast Asia with little or no understanding of where it actually comes from.

item.title: Gunfire Erupts at Philippine Senate Amid Standoff Over ICC Suspect
item.description: Earlier this week, Senator Ronald dela Rosa, the enforcer of President Rodrigo Duterte's "war on drugs," fled to his Senate office to avoid arrest on a warrant from the ICC.

item.title: China Was Ready for the Age of Anarchy
item.description: Why turbulence will make Beijing more assertive.

item.title: The Illusion of Reciprocity
item.description: Trump’s self-defeating trade policy.

item.title: 1MDB Fugitive Jho Low Sought Pardon From US President Trump, Report Claims
item.description: The Malaysian financier recently sought a pardon, despite being accused of masterminding the theft of $4.5 billion from the state investment fund.

item.title: Big tech is sacrificing its cashflows to prop up the AI boom
item.description: The result is increasingly unsettling

item.title: The war between businesses and hackers enters a perilous new phase
item.description: AI agents present novel dangers

item.title: Pakistan’s Biggest Film Is Going to China. The Real Test for Cultural Ties Comes After.
item.description: Can “The Legend of Maula Jatt” win over Chinese audiences and pave the way for more Pakistani films?

item.title: Nepal’s Digital Divide: Why Millions Still Lack Affordable Internet Access
item.description: Despite high reported internet penetration, millions of Nepalis – especially in rural areas – remain offline or struggle with unreliable and unaffordable connections.
